payload.text: An app for mobile automotive and bicycle repair, flat tires and road side repai assistance

**Designing Mobile Repair App**

I'm currently focused on the mobile-friendly React application. The idea is to integrate Veo for dynamic image animation – users can upload photos and generate videos. This will be key for demonstrating repair steps and features. I'm keen to ensure it's intuitive for mobile automotive and bicycle repairs.


**Incorporating New Features**

I'm now incorporating search and map grounding into the app, focusing on the emergency assistance request flow.  I'm particularly interested in parts and technician lookup and the visual section where users will be able to turn images into videos and create AI-generated designs. The file structure is being defined, which includes services for API interactions and types. The chatbot uses Search and Maps, and there's a strong emphasis on responsive design, including a sticky CTA for emergency calls.


**Integrating AI Tools**

I'm now integrating Veo and Nano Banana Pro. The app will feature an AI-driven image-to-video tool using Veo, and generate schematics or modified vehicle images via Nano Banana Pro. I'm focusing on API key management and handling multiple AI modes in state management. I've also established a basic file structure with key components, services, and types. Tailwind CSS is used for setup.


**Specifying Model Integrations**

I'm now zeroing in on integrating the specific AI models.  For Veo, I'm using `veo-3.1-fast-generate-preview`, checking for API key selection, and focusing on polling operation.  For Search, I'm using `gemini-3-flash-preview`, and for Maps, I've selected `gemini-2.5-flash`.  I am going to ensure that image generation uses `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`, and the emergency assistance feature leverages a strong visual hierarchy with urgent colors and large, mobile-friendly buttons.


**Structuring Core Features**

I'm now focusing on core feature structuring. I'm streamlining the process by unifying API key selection and creating a "Key Selection" modal. The app's main tabs will be Chat/Assist (Search & Maps), Visualizer (Image Gen and Veo), and Services (Local repair finder). Veo will use polling. Image generation will use inline data and Maps will use `toolConfig.retrievalConfig.latLng`. Search will use a googleSearch tool. I'm using Tailwind `amber-500` for the app's visual polish.

Application entry point rendering to the DOM.

payload.text: Integrate AI-driven diagnostics that can analyze user-provided vehicle symptoms or error codes to suggest potential problems and repair steps.

**Conceptualizing Diagnostic Integration**

I'm currently focused on integrating "AI-driven diagnostics" into RoadSidePro. The goal is to allow users to input symptoms or codes and receive potential problem diagnoses and suggested repair steps. My thoughts are revolving around how this will interface with existing functionalities like `AppMode.SEARCH` and `AppMode.MAP`. The crucial next step is to map out this new functionality and build an API that utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs).


**Implementing New Diagnostics Mode**

I'm now in the process of implementing the new `AppMode.DIAGNOSE`. I've added it to `types.ts` and updated the navigation in `App.tsx`. I'm leaning towards extending the `ChatInterface` to create a more specialized `DiagnosticInterface` for the UI. The next steps involve adding the `diagnoseAssistant` function and integrating it into the diagnostic logic using `gemini-3-pro-preview` with focused system instructions.


**Developing a Diagnostic Report**

I'm making progress on the structured diagnostic report. Users will input symptoms or error codes, and the AI will provide potential causes, severity levels, and repair steps. I've decided on the visual appearance of the new "Diagnose" tab, with an engine icon, and I'm updating `types.ts` and `services/geminiService.ts` to accommodate the new mode and assistant. I plan to extend the `ChatInterface` to fit this new functionality.


**Prioritizing Mode Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating the `DIAGNOSE` mode into the application. I've updated `types.ts` and realized `ASSIST` was present but unused, and I can now leverage it to be the main diagnostic tool, which means I can also remove the redundant `SEARCH` mode. The immediate steps are updating `geminiService.ts` with `gemini-3-pro-preview` and thinking capabilities. Then, I will update the `ChatInterface` and `App.tsx` components to accommodate this change. This will enable me to analyze user-provided vehicle symptoms or error codes.

entries.description: Add DIAGNOSE to AppMode enum for the new diagnostic functionality.
Implement diagnoseAssistant using Gemini 3 Pro with thinking capabilities for complex automotive troubleshooting.
Update ChatInterface to support the new DIAGNOSE mode and use appropriate diagnostic services.
Add the Diagnose tab to the main navigation and update the rendering logic.

payload.text: In the Vehicle Concept Visualizer, allow users to upload a reference image of a car to influence the AI's generation.

**Developing the Image Integration**

I'm currently focused on integrating the image upload feature into the `Visualizer` component. My primary objective is to enable users to upload reference images that will then inform the AI's vehicle concept generation. The initial task involves updating the `services/geminiService.ts` file to accommodate this image input.


**Refining the Image Integration**

I'm now refining the integration. I'm updating the `generateVehicleImage` signature to accept a `referenceImage` object containing base64 data and a mime type. In `Visualizer.tsx`, I'll add state management for the uploaded image, a hidden file input, and a preview element. I will also incorporate an "Upload Reference" button and the UI is being designed to be automotive-themed.


**Implementing Image Preview**

I've tackled the file selection process, successfully converting the selected image to base64 format. The next step is displaying a thumbnail preview, which is underway. I'll modify the `handleGenerateImage` function to conditionally include the `referenceImage` data if an image is available.

entries.description: Update generateVehicleImage to support an optional reference image part for image-to-image generation.
Add image upload capability to the Visualizer component to allow users to provide reference images for AI generation.

payload.text: Ai will find error code given by user on vehicles dashboard?  Or do they need to purchase a diagnostic tool

**Clarifying Diagnostic Capabilities**

I'm currently focused on the AI's diagnostic abilities. My primary thought is to clarify whether the AI can interpret error codes directly from a vehicle's dashboard or if a separate diagnostic tool is required. I need to explain the current functionality in this context, so I'm outlining how the AI currently processes information related to automotive diagnostics.


**Refining Interpretation Scope**

I'm now zeroing in on the AI's diagnostic limitations. It's becoming clear that the system requires user input: error codes must be provided manually, either typed in or pasted from a scanner. It can't automatically extract data from the vehicle's computer. I'm also confirming the system's ability to analyze user-provided symptoms, sounds, and smells to provide context around the information. The goal is a precise explanation that clarifies the data input process.


**Explaining Current Input Methods**

I've clarified the AI's current data input methods. Users must manually input error codes or describe symptoms. The "Diagnose" feature readily accepts text. I considered, but rejected, expanding the app's capabilities to include image-based code extraction because it would require changes to the application, which is not the goal. I'm focused on accurately describing the app's existing capabilities to the user's needs.


**Considering Visual Input Options**

I'm now thinking about how the AI can handle error code acquisition. I've realized a significant improvement is possible: enabling users to upload dashboard photos for code extraction. The current setup relies on manual input, but the AI is capable of image analysis, which I will incorporate into the workflow. I'm focusing on the technical implications of this update.


**Implementing Visual Data Processing**

I'm now integrating image data into the diagnoseAssistant. I'm updating `geminiService.ts` to accept image uploads and adding an attachment button to `ChatInterface.tsx` to include an image preview. Specifically, I'm making it possible for users to include photos of their dashboards. In this mode, users may snap a photo of their car's dashboard or OBD-II scanner screen for analysis, which improves workflow. I'm also ensuring the AI's system instruction includes visual analysis of relevant components.


**Integrating Image Analysis Capabilities**

I'm expanding the AI's diagnostic capabilities to include image analysis. I'm focusing on the integration of an image input feature, allowing users to upload photos of diagnostic scanner screens. The AI (`gemini-3-pro-preview`) will analyze both the image and any user-provided text to enhance the diagnostic report. The attachment button is designed to be exclusively visible in "Diagnose" mode. Clear indicators will also provide visual feedback that the AI is processing the image.


**Formulating Response to Question**

I've considered how to best address the user's question about the AI's diagnostic capabilities. The user inquiry prompted a quick answer. I believe the app now readily accepts images for error code analysis. The goal is to provide a concise and helpful reply to the user, answering their questions directly about the diagnostic process. I'm focusing on a helpful response.


**Adding Image Code Acquisition**

Now, I'm working to expand the app's error code acquisition. I intend to implement the ability for users to upload or capture photos of their dashboard displays or scanners directly within the "Diagnose" feature. This is in addition to accepting manually typed codes. I'll focus on providing clear instructions on how the image analysis feature works in conjunction with the existing diagnostic flow.


**Specifying Implementation Steps**

I'm now detailing the specific steps to implement image-based diagnostics. I'm updating `diagnoseAssistant` to handle multi-part messages including text and images. Also, I will enhance `ChatInterface` by adding a `diagnosticImage` state and including a file input/button in `DIAGNOSE` mode. I'm focusing on user experience, and visual appearance.

The AI in RoadSidePro can interpret codes you type in, but to make your life easier, I have updated the app to support **Visual Diagnostics**. You can now snap a photo of your car's dashboard or a diagnostic scanner's screen, and the AI will analyze the warning lights and error codes directly from the image.

While a physical OBD-II diagnostic tool is the most accurate way to get deep system data, this AI update allows you to get an expert interpretation of what your dashboard is telling you without needing to purchase any extra hardware for basic troubleshooting.

### Updates made to the app:
1.  **Visual Diagnostic Support**: The `diagnoseAssistant` now uses Gemini 3 Pro's multi-modal capabilities to "see" and analyze photos of dashboards, part labels, or scanner screens.
2.  **Image Attachment UI**: Added a camera/upload button to the diagnostic chat input.
3.  **Contextual Analysis**: The AI now combines visual data from your photo with your text description (e.g., "The car is shaking") to provide a much more accurate repair step-by-step.

entries.description: Enhance diagnoseAssistant to handle image data for visual vehicle diagnostics.
Add image attachment capability to the ChatInterface for visual diagnostics.

payload.text: OK and there is a live call as well for serviceman repairman to come to user to help

**Exploring Live Support Integration**

I'm currently focused on integrating a "Live Call" feature into the application. My goal is to enable direct interaction between users and service professionals for roadside assistance. I'm evaluating how this will mesh with the existing Diagnostics, Search, Maps, and Visualizer functionalities. I'm also considering how the existing Gemini models can be used in this new integration.


**Refining the Live UI**

I've been drilling down on the user interface for the "Live Assist" feature. The central element will be a pulsing microphone icon on the screen, accompanied by real-time transcriptions to show the ongoing conversation. I'm leveraging the Gemini 2.5 Flash Native Audio with `live.connect` for the real-time interaction. I am making progress and need to consider controls for initiating and concluding the live session. I am making rapid progress and will soon move to implementation.


**Developing AI Technician Integration**

I'm now fully immersed in the "AI Rapid Response Technician" concept. The UI details are set: a "Calling Technician..." state during connection, an animated wave during the live session, and an "End Call" button. Implementation will start with the `AppMode` enum in `types.ts`, a new `liveService.ts` for session management, and the `LiveAssist.tsx` component. I'm updating `App.tsx` for navigation. And I need to update the `metadata.json` for microphone permissions. It is time to get to it!

entries.description: Add microphone permission for real-time AI technician calls.
Add LIVE_ASSIST to AppMode enum.
Create the Live Assist component for real-time voice interaction with an AI Technician.
Update main App component to include Live Assist in navigation.

payload.text: In the LiveAssist component, add a text input field and a send button to allow users to type messages to the AI technician, in addition to the voice call.

**Developing Text Interaction**

I'm currently focused on integrating a text input field and a send button into the `LiveAssist` component. The goal is to provide users with a text-based communication option alongside the existing voice call feature. This addition should enhance user interaction with the AI technician within the live assistance context.


**Considering Realtime Input**

I'm now investigating how the Live API handles text input, specifically `session.sendRealtimeInput`. It seems text interaction is usually managed through `chat` or initial configuration text parts. However, for a chat-like experience within a Live session, I'm leaning toward sending a text part as "realtime input." This could be the most effective way to integrate text seamlessly.


**Investigating Realtime Text**

I've been poring over the Live API documentation. It's clear `sendRealtimeInput` is designed for low-latency voice and image data. While the examples don't explicitly showcase in-session text sending, I've confirmed that a text part can be sent, so that's a key point. This could be used for text-based interruptions. I'm now leaning towards using `session.sendRealtimeInput({ text: '...' })` for seamless text integration, and the transcript for chat history. Further, I also noticed the SDK's `LiveClientMessage` contains `clientContent`.


**Defining Visual Integration**

I'm now focusing on UI refinements for the `LiveAssist` component. I'm adding a `chatInput` state, a scrollable chat area, and an input field with a send button. My plan is to use `session.sendRealtimeInput({ text: chatInput })` when sending messages. The transcript will be the chat history, and the text input is only active during the call, as per requirements. I'll need to capture both server and client input in the transcript. I will start by updating `LiveAssist.tsx`. I am also working on visual changes.


**Incorporating Chat History**

I'm now incorporating a `chatHistory` state to meticulously store all messages, both from the user and the AI. I'm also adding a `textInput` state and updating the UI with a scrollable message list and a dedicated text input field with a "Send" button. My plan is to implement a `handleSendMessage` function, which will utilize `sessionRef.current.sendRealtimeInput({ text: textInput })`. Furthermore, I'm working to refine the layout to seamlessly accommodate both the voice visualizer and the text chat interface. This is a crucial element for a fluid user experience. I'm focusing on the SDK's `sendRealtimeInput`, which should correspond to `client_content`.


**Designing Chat Implementation**

I'm now implementing the fundamental chat logic, and I've focused on `sendRealtimeInput` for user input. Upon the user sending a message, I'll need to update chat history manually. The AI's responses will be handled by the existing message handling. I will split the `LiveAssist` component into two parts: the "Voice Hub" on top, and a "Live Chat" panel on the bottom. Further, I will add an input bar. The `LiveServerMessage` handling must be checked with transcription enabled.

To enhance the real-time interaction, I will update the `LiveAssist` component to include a integrated chat interface. This allows users to type specific technical details (like VIN numbers or complex parts names) while maintaining the voice connection with the AI technician.

### Specification
1.  **Unified History**: The transcript area will be transformed into a proper chat history, combining voice-to-text transcriptions and manually typed messages.
2.  **Input Integration**: A dedicated text input field will be added to the bottom of the `LiveAssist` view.
3.  **Real-time Text Transmission**: When a user sends a text message, it will be transmitted through the active Live session using `sendRealtimeInput`, allowing the AI to respond immediately via voice and text.
4.  **Auto-scroll Interface**: The chat container will automatically scroll to the latest message to ensure the user can follow the "live" instructions easily.
5.  **Visual Refinement**: The layout will be adjusted to balance the "Voice Hub" visualizer with the new scrollable chat history.

entries.description: Add a text input field and chat history to LiveAssist for hybrid voice/text technician sessions.

payload.text: Have ai assistant refer user to call on site repairman through the app, integrated through map interface like uber

**Analyzing Integration Possibilities**

Okay, I'm examining the current app's architecture now. The goal is to figure out the best way to integrate a "call a repairman" feature. I'm focusing on a map interface like Uber, and how it can mesh seamlessly with the existing user experience.


**Considering Interface Integration**

I've been honing in on the map interface integration, as the user requested. The `ChatInterface` is pivotal here; it can trigger the switch to the map view. My plan is to update `geminiService.ts` to enable an AI trigger and create a dedicated `DispatchMap` component, similar to Uber. I'm also adding a new `AppMode.DISPATCH` to `types.ts`.


**Implementing Dispatch Features**

I'm now deep-diving into the "Uber-like" dispatch implementation. The focus is on a map component, animated technician markers, and a clear "Request Technician" flow. I am also working on the UI: a full-screen map with a bottom sheet for technician details and progress indicators. I am modifying `types.ts` to include `AppMode.DISPATCH`, updating `App.tsx` for new navigation, developing the `DispatchMap.tsx` component, and updating the chat to suggest dispatches. I can modify the `MAPS` tab or add a feature to it.


**Building the Dispatch System**

I'm making progress now. The map interface is taking shape; a modern aesthetic with pulsing user location, wrench-icon technicians, and sliding panels. I am adding AI-driven dispatch suggestions, which means I've updated the `diagnoseAssistant` system instruction to trigger it. I'm focusing on seamless integration, like the "Request On-Site Help" button that shows in the chat.

payload.text: In the DispatchMap component, display the estimated arrival time for each available technician in a clear, visible format on their marker.

**Visualizing ETA Data**

I'm focusing on enhancing the `DispatchMap` component, specifically integrating ETA data directly onto technician markers. The goal is clear: to ensure the estimated arrival time for each technician is readily apparent on the map, improving the visual clarity of the component.


**Revising Marker Aesthetics**

I'm thinking about making the technician markers more visually distinct. Currently, the ETA is displayed, but I'm considering design adjustments for enhanced legibility. Perhaps a slightly larger badge or a "MINS" label could improve clarity. The goal is to make the ETA easily visible at a glance. I am evaluating different styles for the markers to meet the user requirement.


**Fine-tuning Marker Details**

I am now focusing on the marker's visual details. The implementation, as is, utilizes a small badge with the ETA. To improve clarity, I'm increasing marker size and using high-contrast text and distinct icons. I'm exploring a tail or pin for added location context, and revising the component's code. I'm aiming for an improved visual representation of the ETA.


**Refining ETA Display**

I'm now revising the ETA display within the technician markers, focusing on improving clarity. I've increased the boldness of the ETA text and added a pseudo-element for a "pin" effect, as requested. I'm also ensuring the styling is consistent with the component's design. The changes will be applied in `DispatchMap.tsx`. I will check and modify `geminiService.ts` if needed.

entries.description: Enhance technician markers to display ETA in a more clear and visible high-contrast format.

**Considering App Mode**

I'm currently focused on integrating the AI-driven "Shop" feature. My primary task involves modifying the existing `AppMode`, specifically the "MAPS" mode. I'm exploring how to best update this or create a new dedicated mode within the current navigation flow.
